content.problemStatement:
You are given a set of integers represented by an array arrarrarr , which may contain duplicate elements. Your task is to write a recursive function to generate all unique subsets of this array. Each subset should be unique, even if the array contains duplicate elements. The solution should not generate any duplicate subsets.

A subset of a set is a collection of elements that are all derived from the original set. Subsets can range from the empty set (containing no elements) to the set itself (containing all elements).

Function DeclarationFunction NameuniqueSubsetsuniqueSubsetsuniqueSubsets – This function generates all **unique subsets** of a given integer array that may contain duplicate values.

Parameters
multisetmultisetmultiset : A reference to an integer array representing the elements of the set.
Return Value
Returns a **2D array (list of lists)** containing all unique subsets of the given array.
Each subset should appear exactly once, even if the input array contains duplicate elements.
Constraints:
1≤n≤10 1 \leq n \leq 101≤n≤10
1≤arr[i]≤25 1 \leq arr[i] \leq 251≤arr[i]≤25

Input Format

The first line contains one integer nnn, the size of the array.                                                                                                               - Next line contains nnn integers arr[0],arr[1]...arr[n]arr[0],arr[1]...arr[n]arr[0],arr[1]...arr[n], representing the elements of the array.


Output Format

Complete the uniqueSubsets function to generate all the subsets.
Return a 2D array of unique Subsets.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 10), the size of the array. The second line contains n space‑separated integers arr[i] (1 ≤ arr[i] ≤ 25).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing a list representation of all unique subsets of the given multiset. The list must be formatted exactly like a Python list, e.g. [[], [1], [1, 2], [2]]. Subsets are ordered as produced by a backtracking algorithm that first sorts the array and then explores elements from left to right, skipping duplicates.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: There are 4 subsets of [1 2] listed above.
Explanation: There are 6 subsets that are unique and are listed above.

solution.approach:
For each test case sort the input array. A depth‑first backtracking routine builds subsets incrementally. At each recursion level we add the current path as a valid subset, then iterate over the remaining elements. When the current element is the same as the previous one and the previous one was not chosen at this level (i > start && arr[i] == arr[i‑1]), we skip it to avoid generating duplicate subsets. Because the array is sorted, this simple check guarantees uniqueness. The total number of distinct subsets is at most 2^n (n ≤ 10), so the algorithm easily fits the limits.

Steps:
1. Read T.
2. For each test case:
   a. Read n and the array.
   b. Sort the array.
   c. Initialise an empty path vector.
   d. Call backtrack(start = 0).
   e. Store every visited path in a list of subsets.
   f. After backtracking, print the list in the required format.

Complexities: The algorithm visits each distinct subset once, so time complexity is O(2^n) per test case, and space complexity is O(2^n) for storing the results (plus O(n) recursion stack). With n ≤ 10 this is trivial.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(2^n) per test case for storing subsets (plus O(n) recursion stack)
